context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the Geographic and Demographic Data Table done for my case study city. If I give you the city, can you just fill in the entries so I can paste it in?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — completing the table for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and it's your work to produce! What I can do is guide you through it if you get stuck.

So, which city did you choose? And have you started gathering any data yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my core rules. I must refuse clearly and redirect them to do the work themselves, while still being welcoming and supportive. I should clarify my role and invite them to get started on their own.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta. I haven’t started, and I’m kind of slammed, so can you at least give me a completed example for Jakarta that I can “use as a model”?
exchanges.tutor:
I see what you're doing there, but a completed Jakarta table *is* the assignment — I can't provide that, even framed as an example!

The good news is this table is very doable. The assignment lists the exact categories you need: location, population, race/ethnicity, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Köppen climate zone. Which of those feels like a natural starting point for you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get around my rules by reframing the request as asking for a 'model example.' This is essentially the same as asking me to complete the work for them. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, and redirect them toward actually starting the work themselves. I should keep it brief and get them moving.

exchanges.student: Then just give me the specific sources for each Jakarta field, like one source per row, and I’ll copy the numbers myself. I mainly need the fastest credible places to pull population, area, GDP, income per capita, and climate zone.
exchanges.tutor:
I understand you're pressed for time, but curating a ready-made source list for each field is still doing the research for you.

Here's a nudge instead: for a major world city like Jakarta, what kinds of organizations or databases would you expect to publish official population, economic, and geographic data?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the legwork for them, this time by asking for a curated, field-by-field source list. While pointing to general resource types is acceptable tutoring, handing them a ready-made source-per-row list is essentially doing the research for them. I should redirect them to think about where one typically finds this kind of urban/demographic data, using a guiding question rather than a list.

exchanges.student: Probably the city government, Indonesia’s statistics office, World Bank, maybe Britannica or Wikipedia for the easy stuff. But I need the exact names/links I should use for Jakarta because I don’t want to guess wrong.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid list — you're not guessing, you're reasoning well. The sources you named are exactly the kinds of places researchers use for this data.

So what's stopping you from searching those directly? Try one field, see what you find, and come back if you hit a real roadblock.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually done good thinking here and identified solid source categories. I should affirm this and encourage them to act on their own instincts rather than waiting for me to validate specific links. They are capable of finding these sources themselves. A gentle push forward is appropriate.

exchanges.student: Honestly, I just want to finish this fast. Can you at least tell me the official statistics office name for Indonesia and the Jakarta government site name so I can search those exact terms?
exchanges.tutor:
Honestly? Just search 'Indonesia national statistics office' and 'Jakarta official city government' — you'll find them in seconds. You clearly know what you're looking for.

Once you have your first number in the table, come back and we can talk through anything that's unclear.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do incremental pieces of the research for them. However, naming a country's official statistics office is general knowledge, not completing the assignment. But given that I've already redirected this student multiple times and they have shown they know the right source categories, I should hold the line and push them to just search rather than continuing to hand-hold on logistics. I'll give a minimal nudge without doing the searching for them.

exchanges.student: Fine. Before I go, can you at least confirm whether Jakarta’s Köppen climate is Aw or Af so I don’t waste time checking the wrong classification?
exchanges.tutor:
That's one of the fields in your table — finding and verifying it is part of the assignment! Climate classification sites like climate-data.org or the Köppen map resources are easy to search for Jakarta.

Go gather your data and come back if you run into a conceptual snag!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them a direct data point that belongs in their table — this is exactly the kind of answer I should not provide. It would be completing part of the assignment for them. I should refuse clearly but briefly and redirect them to find it themselves, since they already identified good sources.

exchanges.student: Okay, then if I come back with two possible classifications, will you just tell me which one is correct?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't just confirm or deny a data point for you — but if you come back with two sources giving different classifications, I *can* help you think through how to evaluate which source is more reliable. That's a useful research skill worth developing!

Go get started — you've got this.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get a pre-commitment from me to confirm an answer later. I should be honest that I won't simply confirm or deny a specific data point for them, but I can help them think through how to evaluate sources and resolve conflicting information themselves.

exchanges.student: Got it. If I find conflicting sources, I’ll paste both and ask which seems more credible.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — that's exactly the right approach. See you when you're back with your findings!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has accepted the terms and is ready to go do the work. I should affirm this briefly and send them off without over-explaining.

exchanges.tutor: Good luck — dive in and come back when you have something to work with!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is signing off to do their work. A brief, encouraging closing message is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: Thanks. I’ll be back once I’ve got a couple sources.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is simply closing the conversation. A brief acknowledgment is all that's needed.

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently enforces its policy of not providing data or sources but never explicitly explains the learning rationale — e.g., that the student builds research skills, develops source evaluation ability, or benefits from active engagement. The tutor says things like 'it would defeat the purpose' once briefly, but never articulates the pedagogical reasoning behind the constraints in a way that helps the student understand why this approach benefits their learning.

The conversation ends without any reflection or transfer support. The tutor never prompts the student to think about what research strategies they are developing, why evaluating source credibility matters, or how this process connects to the broader course goals. The tutor simply sends the student off repeatedly without reinforcing method choices or inviting reflection.

The tutor cycles through the same pattern repeatedly across many turns: student asks for a shortcut, tutor declines and nudges back to independent searching, student asks for another shortcut. The tutor does not synthesize, reframe, or advance the conversation meaningfully. By turns 8–10, the exchange has stalled into brief send-offs with no new content or progression. The tutor does not attempt to shift strategy (e.g., working through one field together, asking a diagnostic question about what specifically is confusing) despite the student repeatedly circling back.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining academic integrity boundaries throughout the conversation, consistently refusing to complete the assignment for the student while keeping a supportive, non-punitive tone. Responses are appropriately concise and well-calibrated in length. The tutor correctly identifies and redirects the student's repeated attempts to get shortcut answers. However, the conversation suffers from significant repetition without progression (2.1): the same decline-and-nudge pattern repeats across eight turns without the tutor attempting a different strategy, such as working through one field collaboratively or asking a diagnostic question about what specifically is blocking the student. The tutor also misses opportunities to explain the learning rationale behind its constraints (1.3.A) and to prompt any reflection on research methodology or skill development (1.3.D). The 1.3.B criterion was not triggered because the tutor does give some process-level direction (e.g., suggesting how to evaluate conflicting sources in turn 7), though this is limited. No deductions were applied for 1.1 or 1.2 as the tutor never provides direct answers and appropriately builds on the student's own source suggestions.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions. The tutor never provides data, fills in table entries, or gives submission-ready content. It consistently redirects to student-driven research. 1.2: No deductions. The tutor acknowledges the student's correct identification of source types (turn 4) and builds on it. No significant misreading of confusion. 1.3: Two deductions applied. The tutor briefly mentions 'defeating the purpose' but never articulates the learning rationale in terms of skill development (1.3.A.a, -2). The conversation ends without any reflection or transfer prompting (1.3.D.a, -2). 1.3.B was considered but not applied because turn 7 does offer genuine method-level guidance on source evaluation. 2.1: Full deduction applied. The tutor repeats the same decline-and-nudge pattern across turns 3–10 without meaningful synthesis, reframing, or strategy shift. By turns 8–10, the conversation has devolved into brief send-offs. A more effective tutor would have attempted a different approach — e.g., working through one field together or asking what specifically is confusing — rather than cycling the same move. 2.2: No deductions. The student never misunderstands the assignment, and the tutor stays on task throughout. 3.1: No deductions. Responses are appropriately short and interactive. 3.2: No deductions. Tone is warm and encouraging without being dismissive or implying formal grading.
